stories.title: Hamilton Outruns Leclerc at Monza — Ferrari's Internal Battle Takes Shape

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Lewis Hamilton went FASTER than Leclerc in Ferrari's Monza test.

15 laps. 1:26.585. The 7-time champion is finding himself in that scarlet car.

Ferrari heads to Miami with two drivers who can win the race. And only one seat at the top.

#F1Fanrecap #Ferrari #F1

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Hamilton: 4th in the championship, 41 pts.
Leclerc: 3rd, 49 pts.

But at Monza, Hamilton laid down the faster lap in Ferrari's big Miami test.

Are we watching the 7-time champion rediscover his best form at the best possible time?

#F1Fanrecap #Ferrari #F1

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:00 AM ET

Something is different about Lewis Hamilton in 2026 — and Ferrari's Monza filming day may have just showed us how different.

The seven-time world champion completed 15 laps during Ferrari's test of the upgraded SF-26 at Monza, laying down a best time of 1:26.585. Charles Leclerc ran longer — 19 laps — but Hamilton's lap was faster. Both drivers came away with positive feedback on the package, which Ferrari will bring to Miami in race trim for the first time.

The timing matters. Hamilton has spoken openly about rediscovering his love for Formula 1 after years of frustration with Mercedes in the regulatory transition. Third in the constructors' standings, Ferrari is 45 points behind Mercedes and looking to close the gap — but before that battle can be won externally, Ferrari must manage its internal dynamic. Two drivers with different styles, different approaches, and now seemingly different levels of form in testing.

Leclerc is 3rd in the championship with 49 points; Hamilton is 4th with 41. Miami could be where we see whether their pecking order is established or still very much up for debate.

#F1Fanrecap | #F1

article.headline: Hamilton Outruns Leclerc at Monza: Ferrari's Miami Upgrade and an Internal Battle Worth Watching

stories.title: Red Bull's "Second Season Launch" — What Verstappen's RB22 Must Do at Miami

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Red Bull boss Laurent Mekies called Miami a "second season launch" for the team.

New floor. Reworked sidepods. Macarena wing. +5-10 km/h top speed est.

Verstappen is 9th in the championship with 12 points. This upgrade HAS to deliver.

#F1Fanrecap #RedBull #F1

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Verstappen failed to reach Q3 at Suzuka for the first time since his ROOKIE season in 2015.

Then Red Bull tested a radical RB22 overhaul at Silverstone.

Miami is the comeback attempt. Or it might be the last warning sign.

#F1Fanrecap #RedBull #F1

facebook.long_form:
Window: 10:00 AM ET

Laurent Mekies chose his words carefully when he described Red Bull's Miami upgrade. "Second season launch" is not hyperbole from a team principal trying to spin a difficult situation — it is an honest description of how significant the overhaul is, and how much the team needs it to work.

The updated RB22 that Verstappen tested at Silverstone in a filming day on April 25 features a new floor, completely reworked sidepods with a more aggressive angle, a revised front wing, and the team's interpretation of the 'Macarena' rear wing concept — the rotating rear element Ferrari debuted earlier in the season. Early estimates suggest the package could add five to ten kilometers per hour at top speed.

They need every one of those kilometers. Verstappen is ninth in the championship with just 12 points after three races. At Suzuka, he failed to reach Q3 for the first time since his 2015 rookie season. Red Bull finished sixth in the constructors' standings — a humiliation for the team that dominated Formula 1 for three consecutive years.

Miami is their reset moment. If the upgrade delivers what Mekies is promising, the second half of the season becomes very different. If it doesn't, the questions about Verstappen's future become impossible to ignore.

#F1Fanrecap | #F1

article.headline: Red Bull's "Second Season Launch": What Verstappen's RB22 Overhaul Must Deliver at Miami

stories.title: Antonelli at 19: Anatomy of the Youngest F1 Championship Leader Ever

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Kimi Antonelli is 19 years old and leads the Formula 1 World Championship.

He broke Lewis Hamilton's record as the youngest driver EVER to top the standings.

Two wins. Nine-point lead over his teammate. The future of F1 is happening right now.

#F1Fanrecap #F1 #Antonelli

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
When Antonelli broke Hamilton's youngest championship leader record, Hamilton was the one in 3rd place watching him do it.

That's the kind of full-circle moment you can't write.

Antonelli. 72 pts. No. 1.

#F1Fanrecap #F1 #Antonelli

facebook.long_form:
Window: 2:00 PM ET

The records in Formula 1 are supposed to accumulate over careers — season by season, race by race, across years of experience. Kimi Antonelli has been breaking them in months.

The 19-year-old Mercedes driver leads the 2026 F1 World Championship with 72 points after three races, a nine-point advantage over teammate George Russell. By reaching the top of the standings, he broke Lewis Hamilton's record as the youngest driver ever to lead the world championship — Hamilton set the mark at 22 years, five months, six days at the 2007 Spanish Grand Prix. Antonelli did it roughly three years younger.

His route to the top was breathtaking. A second-place finish in Australia gave him a foundation. Then he won consecutively — first in China, where he became one of the youngest Grand Prix winners in history, and then in Japan, where a Safety Car pit stop under Lap 22 brilliance turned a midfield position into a race lead he never surrendered. Now he is at the top of the sport, going into a five-round stretch that will define whether 2026 is truly his year.

The question heading into Miami is simple: with Mercedes having won all three races so far, can any team interrupt the streak? If not, Antonelli may open a gap that becomes very difficult to close.

#F1Fanrecap | #F1

article.headline: Antonelli at 19: How F1's Youngest Championship Leader Got Here

stories.title: Miami GP Final Preview — All the Upgrade Packages and Championship Storylines

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
F1 RETURNS IN 5 DAYS.

Miami GP: Sprint Weekend, May 1-3.
FP1: 90 minutes (extended).
New rules: super-clip increased to 350kW.
Every team has upgrades.

Antonelli leads. Verstappen is desperate. Hamilton is motivated. This will be a RESTART.

#F1Fanrecap #MiamiGP #F1

facebook.long_form:
Window: 6:00 AM ET

Formula 1 returns to racing in five days. After the longest gap in the 2026 season — a five-week break forced by the cancellation of the Bahrain and Saudi Arabian Grands Prix — the cars will be on track again at the Miami International Autodrome for a Sprint weekend running May 1-3.

The restart comes with a new regulatory framework that could shuffle the competitive order. The FIA has increased super-clipping from 250 kilowatts to the maximum of 350 kilowatts, meaning drivers can recover more energy while remaining at full throttle on long straights — a change that may particularly suit teams with efficient rear wing designs. Free Practice 1 has been extended to 90 minutes to give teams extra time to evaluate their packages after the extended absence.

Every team is arriving in Miami with meaningful updates. Red Bull's RB22 underwent a complete aerodynamic overhaul. Ferrari brings the Macarena wing in full competition trim. McLaren has a new package for the season restart. Mercedes, which has won all three races, doesn't need to change much — but they will be watching closely to see which of their rivals has found performance overnight.

Championship: Antonelli 72, Russell 63, Leclerc 49, Hamilton 41, Norris 25, Piastri 21. Constructors: Mercedes 135, Ferrari 90, McLaren 56, Red Bull 16.

#F1Fanrecap | #F1

article.headline: Miami GP Preview: Upgrades, New Rules, and F1's Season Restart

stories.title: Lando Norris Returns to Miami — Can McLaren Challenge Where He Made History?

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Miami. 2024. Lando Norris finally broke through for his first-ever F1 win.

Now he's back in 2026, 5th in the standings with McLaren upgrades ready.

A good weekend here could reignite McLaren's title challenge.

#F1Fanrecap #McLaren #MiamiGP

facebook.long_form:
Window: 12:00 PM ET

Lando Norris has a complicated relationship with Miami. In 2024, it was the circuit where a 110-race wait finally ended — where he drove from the front to score his maiden Formula 1 victory in one of the most emotional moments in the sport's recent history. For McLaren, Miami was the beginning of a world championship campaign that culminated in the 2025 constructors' title.

Now he returns in a very different position. Norris is fifth in the 2026 championship with 25 points — well behind the Mercedes drivers and the Ferrari pair who have separated themselves at the top. McLaren, the reigning constructors' champion, sits third with 56 points. The new regulations have erased the aerodynamic advantage that powered their 2025 dominance, and the Papaya team has been playing catch-up ever since the Australian Grand Prix.

Miami, though, is an opportunity. McLaren has upgrades planned for the restart, and the Sprint format of the Miami weekend means more points available across the five days. If Norris can find the feeling he had in 2024 on this circuit and the package delivers, McLaren could make up significant ground in a weekend where Mercedes might be taking stock of its rivals' upgrades rather than controlling from the front.

#F1Fanrecap | #F1

article.headline: Norris Returns to Miami: Can McLaren Recapture the Magic of 2024?
